{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.4, "R1.3": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.95}}

Polished the section by cutting hedging, removing repetition, and tightening prose; flagged a few places where the original claims may need numerical support you should verify.

---

We report the performance of our fine-tuned protein language model on variant effect prediction across three benchmark panels. The fine-tuned model yields a modest but consistent improvement over the baseline ESM-2 checkpoint, though the margin varies with the subset of variants considered.

On ClinVar, the fine-tuned model reaches a Spearman correlation of 0.612, compared with 0.594 for the baseline (+0.018). On the DMS-substitution panel, it reaches 0.581 versus 0.566 (+0.015). On the ProteinGym indel panel, it reaches 0.498 versus 0.481 (+0.017). The improvements are of comparable magnitude across all three panels.

The fine-tuned model also appears more sample-efficient: it requires fewer labeled variants to reach a correlation comparable to the baseline trained on the full set. This effect is sensitive to the sampling strategy, and we have not characterized it exhaustively across the low-data regime.

The reported gains are stable across random seeds used to initialize the adapter weights. Per-residue attribution maps are consistent with previously reported binding-site annotations, suggesting that fine-tuning captures biologically meaningful signal rather than dataset-specific artifacts.

---

Notes on substance (not just prose):
- The original used "modest improvement over the baseline" three times in one paragraph — collapsed into a single summary sentence plus explicit deltas.
- Stacked hedges ("may potentially," "could possibly") were removed. Where genuine uncertainty exists (sample-efficiency claim, attribution-map interpretation), one hedge is retained.
- The sample-efficiency paragraph asserts a behavior without reporting any numbers; if you have a learning-curve figure or a "N labels to match baseline" statistic, insert it — otherwise this paragraph remains the weakest evidentially.
- Consider adding confidence intervals or seed variance (e.g., "0.612 ± 0.004") to substantiate "stable across seeds"; the current text asserts stability without quantifying it.

Uncertainty: I did not verify whether your reported numbers (0.612, 0.594, etc.) are accurate, whether the deltas are statistically significant, or whether the attribution-map claim is supported by an analysis elsewhere in the paper. The arithmetic in the deltas (+0.018, +0.015, +0.017) is mine — please double-check.
